skill.name: gasp-diagnostics
skill.description: Diagnose Linux system health by fetching and interpreting GASP metrics from local hosts or provided JSON output.
skill.summary: Analyze GASP monitoring data for Linux performance and reliability issues.
skill.author: AcceleratedIndustries

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command and crypto patterns, but most are false positives from markdown code spans, formulas, and diagnostic text. Confirmed risks are intentional network access to user-specified GASP endpoints and an optional curl or wget helper script. No evidence found of prompt injection, malicious persistence, credential exfiltration, or automatic destructive command execution.

medium_findings.title: User-Directed HTTP Metric Fetching
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs assistants to fetch http://{hostname}:8080/metrics and supports hostnames or IP addresses supplied by users. This is intentional for diagnostics, but it can probe internal hosts or retrieve unauthenticated metrics when the assistant has network access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network-fetch behavior is explicit and central to the skill. The risk is contextual because the same behavior is also the documented diagnostic purpose.

medium_findings.title: Optional Helper Script Performs Network Fetches
medium_findings.description: scripts/fetch-gasp.sh constructs an HTTP URL from command-line arguments and sends it to curl or wget. Arguments are quoted and no shell command injection was found, but the caller controls the network target.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script clearly performs outbound HTTP requests to a constructed URL. The command arguments are quoted, so the finding is about network scope rather than command injection.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Findings Are Installation Symlinks
low_findings.description: The README symlinks the skill into ~/.claude/skills as installation guidance. This writes under the user home directory, but it is visible setup documentation and not hidden persistence.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The symlink commands are shown in a documented installation section. They do not run automatically and do not conceal behavior from the user.

low_findings.title: Static Command and Crypto Hits Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: Many static hits are markdown examples, metric names, thresholds, or diagnostic command suggestions such as systemctl, journalctl, and docker exec. No evidence found of automatic command execution or weak cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed context shows diagnostic text and examples rather than executable code paths. Confidence is below very high because the static results include many duplicate pattern matches across documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Active Web Fetch to User-Specified Host
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill directs assistants to fetch a metrics endpoint from a hostname chosen in the user conversation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is explicit and reachable whenever a user asks for a system check. It is expected behavior, but it expands network access risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: curl or wget Against Constructed URL
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper script builds an HTTP URL from input and retrieves it with curl or wget.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code path is direct and uses the caller-provided host and port. Quoting reduces injection risk but not arbitrary network-request risk.

content.user_title: Diagnose Linux Hosts with GASP Metrics
content.value_statement: Linux performance problems are hard to triage from raw monitoring data. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code fetch or interpret GASP metrics and turn them into clear diagnostic findings.
actual_capabilities: Fetch GASP metrics from hostnames, local names, or IP addresses over HTTP.
Interpret CPU load, memory pressure, disk I/O, network, GPU, process, service, and log metrics.
Compare multiple hosts and identify outliers against expected baselines.
Analyze pasted GASP JSON output when network fetching is not needed.
Recommend diagnostic follow-up steps for common Linux performance issues.
limitations: Requires GASP to be running and reachable on the target host.
Uses unauthenticated HTTP examples, so network exposure must be controlled by the user.
Does not replace administrator judgment for restarts, service changes, or data deletion.
May need additional context about expected workloads to avoid false alarms.

use_cases.title: Triage a Slow Workstation
use_cases.description: Identify whether CPU saturation, memory pressure, disk I/O, GPU load, or a specific process explains poor performance.

use_cases.title: Compare Homelab or Proxmox Nodes
use_cases.description: Fetch metrics from several hosts and find the node that differs from expected resource patterns.

use_cases.title: Investigate Service Instability
use_cases.description: Use failed units, restart history, log summaries, and resource metrics to narrow service failure causes.

prompt_templates.title: Check One Host
prompt_templates.prompt: Check {hostname} with GASP and tell me if it is healthy. Focus on the most important issue first.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Pasted Metrics
prompt_templates.scenario: Use provided GASP output without network access.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this GASP output and summarize health, likely causes, and safe next steps: {paste_metrics_here}

prompt_templates.title: Compare Several Hosts
prompt_templates.scenario: Find the outlier across a small group of systems.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare {host_a}, {host_b}, and {host_c}. Identify outliers, shared symptoms, and the most likely root cause.

prompt_templates.title: Investigate a Regression
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow using trends and recent changes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Investigate why {hostname} became slower after {change_or_time}. Correlate recent changes, baselines, trends, and top resource consumers.

output_examples.output: Issue found: hyperion is CPU saturated relative to its core count. The main contributor is a build process, so wait for completion or reduce parallel jobs.

output: proxmox1 matches its expected virtualization baseline.
proxmox2 is the outlier with high memory pressure and recent VM migration activity.
Next step: check VM memory allocation and recent migrations on proxmox2.

output_examples.input: Here is pasted GASP output from my database host.
output_examples.output: The database host shows memory growth above baseline with swap activity. Review long-running queries and restart only after confirming it is safe.

best_practices: Confirm the target hostname before allowing network fetches.
Provide workload context, recent changes, and expected host role when available.
Treat recommendations as diagnostic guidance and approve operational changes separately.
anti_patterns: Do not expose GASP metrics endpoints to untrusted networks.
Do not ask the assistant to fetch unknown hosts without confirming authorization.
Do not restart services or containers based only on one metric spike.

faq.question: What is GASP?
faq.answer: GASP is a monitoring format designed to summarize Linux system metrics for AI-assisted diagnostics.

faq.question: Can this skill fetch live metrics?
faq.answer: Yes. It can fetch HTTP metrics from a named host when the assistant has network access and the user authorizes the target.

faq.question: Can it work without network access?
faq.answer: Yes. You can paste GASP JSON output, and the skill can analyze the provided data.

faq.question: Is this safe for production systems?
faq.answer: It can support production triage, but metric endpoints should be protected and operational changes need human approval.

faq.question: Does it execute repair commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. The skill provides diagnostic recommendations and command examples, but it does not automatically change systems.

faq.question: Which tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
